coredata.dc:title: Transcriptomic and metabolomic investigation of molecular inactivation mechanisms in Escherichia coli triggered by graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Yan
Wu, Wanfeng
Zhao, Yan
Liu, Haoqiang
Li, Zongda
Li, Xinbo
Zhang, Minwei
Qin, Yanan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, are considered potential candidates in the field of biomedical and food safety due to their outstanding antimicrobial properties and excellent biocompatibility. To uncover the molecular regulatory mechanisms underlying the phenotypes, the overall regulation of genes and metabolites in Escherichia coli (E. coli) after GQDs stimulation was investigated by RNA-sequencing and LC-MS. Gene transcription and metabolite expression related to a series of crucial biomolecular processes were influenced by the GQDs stimulation, including biofilm formation, bacterial secretion system, sulfur metabolism and nitrogen metabolism, etc. This study could provide profound insights into the GQDs stress response in E. coli, which would be useful for the development and application of GQDs in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Escherichia coli
Transcriptomic
Metabolomic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Total RNA isolation, cDNA library construction, and sequencing
Data analysis, differentially expressed genes (DEGs) screening, and bioinformatics analysis
Metabolite extraction, LC‒MS-based untargeted metabolomics analysis
Data analysis, differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs) screening, and bioinformatics analysis
Global transcriptomic and metabolomic responses of E. coli to GQDs
GQDs up-regulated transcripts involved in defense mechanisms
GQDs down-regulated transcripts involved in biofilm formation
GQDs regulated transcripts involved in sulfur metabolism and nitrogen metabolism
GQDs up-regulated transcripts involved in bacterial secretion system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs. (a) The AFM images of GQDs. (b) UV–vis spectra of GQDs a concentration of 40 μg mL−1. (c) Zeta Potential of GQDs. (d) FTIR spectra of GQDs. (e) XPS spectra of GQDs. (f) XPS spectra of O 1s orbits. (g) XPS spectra of N 1s orbits. (h) XPS spectra of C 1s orbits.

Antibacterial properties of GQDs. Images of the bacterial colonies formed by (a) S. aureus and (b) E. coli treated with different concentrations (0–600 μg mL−1) of GQDs. Percentage survival rate of (c) S. aureus, (d) E. coli, data points are reported as mean ± standard deviation.

Transcriptomic and metabolic profiling reveals altered gene transcription patterns in E.coli upon GQDs-exposure treatment. (a) Heat map of DEGs in GQDs-treated E.coli cells (control: EA-1, EA-2, EA-3, experimental: EZ-1, EZ-2, EZ-3). (b) Heat map of DEMs in GQDs-treated E.coli cells (control: EC1, EC2, EC3, EC4, EC5, EC6, experimental: ED1, ED2, ED3, ED4, ED5, ED6). The rows represent individual genes/metabolites. Different colors represent different relative abundances of genes/metabolites, where red represents higher intensity and blue represents lower intensity. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Heat map of DEGs (control: EA-1, EA-2, EA-3, experimental: EZ-1, EZ-2, EZ-3) involved in defense mechanisms. (b) DEGs are annotated into two-component system pathway.
(a) Heat map of DEGs (control: EA-1, EA-2, EA-3, experimental: EZ-1, EZ-2, EZ-3) involved in Biofilm formation, (b) DEGs annotated into Escherichia coli biofilm formation pathway.

(a) Heat map of DEGs (control: EA-1, EA-2, EA-3, experimental: EZ-1, EZ-2, EZ-3) involved in sulfur metabolism and nitrogen metabolism. (b) DEGs annotated into (Ⅰ) sulfur metabolism and (Ⅱ) nitrogen metabolism pathway. Genes colored in red or green represent respectively a higher or lower level in GQDs stressed E. coli. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Heat map of DEGs (control: EA-1, EA-2, EA-3, experimental: EZ-1, EZ-2, EZ-3) involved in bacterial secretion system. (b) DEGs annotated into Sec-secretion system pathway.

head.ce:title: Transcriptomic and metabolomic investigation of molecular inactivation mechanisms in Escherichia coli triggered by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science & Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science & Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Biological Resources and Genetic Engineering, Urumqi, 830017, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Biological Resources and Genetic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinjiang Key Laboratory of Biological Resources and Genetic Engineering, Urumqi 830017, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Life Science & Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, China.
Corresponding author. College of Life Science & Technology, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830017, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, are considered potential candidates in the field of biomedical and food safety due to their outstanding antimicrobial properties and excellent biocompatibility. To uncover the molecular regulatory mechanisms underlying the phenotypes, the overall regulation of genes and metabolites in Escherichia coli (E. coli) after GQDs stimulation was investigated by RNA-sequencing and LC-MS. Gene transcription and metabolite expression related to a series of crucial biomolecular processes were influenced by the GQDs stimulation, including biofilm formation, bacterial secretion system, sulfur metabolism and nitrogen metabolism, etc. This study could provide profound insights into the GQDs stress response in E. coli, which would be useful for the development and application of GQDs in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial adaptations and responses to physicochemical interventions lead to difficulties and complexities in food safety assurance, which may expose consumers to an increased risk of foodborne disease (Machado-Moreira et al., 2019). Although synthetic preservatives have been widely commercialized in recent years, a range of environmental and health concerns have emerged from the use of antimicrobial agents, such as severe drug resistance and adverse side effects in consumers (Mostafidi et al., 2020; Chinchkar et al., 2022). As the risk of food contamination expands, concerns regarding extremely toxic synthetic preservatives must shift to novel safer alternatives. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore new and efficient antimicrobial agents.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are one of the recent entrants to the list of carbon-based nanomaterials. Given that GQDs exhibit extraordinary quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs can exhibit enhanced electrocatalytic, electrochemical, and photochemical activities, providing advantages in biosensor and optoelectronic applications over the past decade (Cheng et al., 2022; Ghaffarkhah et al., 2022). Notably, the antibacterial activity of GQDs has received considerable attention in recent years, especially their smaller transverse size, lower toxicity, and better biocompatibility, which have promising applications in the biomedical and food safety fields (Wang et al., 2016b; Henna and Pramod, 2020). GQDs appear to be promising bacteriostatic agents that inhibit bacterial growth (Kuo et al., 2017), previous study demonstrated that GQDs catalyzed the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and induced oxidative damage, and we further discovered that GQDs caused adverse changes in cell wall/membrane permeability and integrity (Wu et al., 2022). Despite these advances, knowledge regarding the pathogen response to antibacterial agents is essential for the optimization and design of novel targeted antibacterial agents.

Emerging omics techniques, such as transcriptomics (Lamas et al., 2019), and metabolomics (Pan et al., 2021) have revolutionized biological scientific research by providing a viable approach to obtain global physicochemical profiles of microorganisms. To date, the molecular mechanisms of bacterial inactivation induced by prolonged cold stress (Li et al., 2018), essential oils (Hu et al., 2021), etc. have been investigated by transcriptome analysis. However, transcriptional analysis usually offer an incomplete view of the metabolic changes resulting from posttranscriptional regulation. Metabolomics provides distinct advantages and is more closely related to the phenotype, which reflects the series changes in the last step induced by an external stimulus. A combined transcriptomic and metabolomic analysis has been recently applied to elucidate the bacterial molecular inactivation mechanisms stimulated by natural plant ingredients (such as persimmon tannin (Liu et al., 2020), thyme and cinnamon oli (Chen et al., 2022), nanomaterials (such as graphene oxide (Wang et al., 2019b), and other stresses (ohmic heating (Shao et al., 2021), etc.) from a global perspective. Therefore, integrated transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses were employed to investigate the molecular regulatory mechanism of E. coli subjected to GQDs in this study.

GQDs were synthesized according to a previous report (Zhang et al., 2017). The synthesized GQDs were then subjected to atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dension Icon, Germany) observation, and the height profile, thickness, and size of the samples were determined by AFM. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of GQDs between 200 nm and 800 nm was measured by a UV–vis spectrometer (Mapada, UV-1200). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Nicolet IS10, Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo, USA) were used to examine the surface chemistry of the materials. The zeta potential was recorded by a spectrometer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90).

The Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli MC1061 and Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus ATCC6538 (Huayueyang Biotech Co., Ltd. Beijing, China) were used to evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of GQDs. Individual colonies of E. coli and S. aureus on solid Luria-Bertani (LB) agar plates were selected and incubated in 5 mL LB liquid medium at 37 °C under 150 rpm rotation for 24 h. Then, 500 μL of bacterial suspension were then inoculated into a new 5 mL liquid medium and incubated until the logarithmic expectation. Subsequently, the bacterial suspensions (OD600 = 0.5) were transferred to 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes with GQDs (the final concentration of GQDs was 50–600 μg mL−1) or phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.2, 0.01 M/L). After incubation at 37 °C for 60 min, the above culture solution was diluted 10,000-fold with PBS. 50 μL of the diluted suspension was inoculated onto the LB agar plates, the plates were cultivated in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The antimicrobial activity of GQDs was assessed using the plate counting method, which determined the number of colony-forming units (CFUs) on the agar plates. The survival rate was calculated according to the following formula.
(1)
P e r c e n t a g e
s u r v i v a l
( % )
=
C F U
o f
G Q D s
C F U
o f
P B S
× 100 %

The E. coli suspensions cultured to logarithmic expectation were diluted with PBS (pH = 7.2, 0.01 M/L) to OD600 = 0.5. Subsequently, 500 μL of the bacterial suspension were transferred to a 1.5 mL centrifuge tube with GQDs (final concentration of 400 μg mL−1) or PBS. The PBS-treated bacterial suspension served as a control. After incubation at 37 °C for 60 min, the supernatant was removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and the bacterial cells were washed three times with PBS. The bacterial cells were then fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 4 °C for 4 h. The fixed bacterial cells were washed three times with PBS and then dehydrated with a series of 30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 96% and 100% alcohols for 10 min. The samples were dried at the critical point of CO2 and placed on a coverslip coated with gold-palladium using an ion coater. Finally, the morphological characteristics of the bacterial cells were observed by SEM (Hitachi SU8020, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total RNA isolation, cDNA library construction, and sequencing
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli was treated with 400 μg mL−1 GQDs for 60 min, and bacterial suspensions treated with PBS served as controls. The bacterial precipitation was obtained via centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with PBS. Three biological replicates of each group (control: EA-1, EA-2, EA-3, experimental: EZ-1, EZ-2, EZ-3) were performed under identical conditions. The High quality RNA was utilized for the cDNA library construction according to Illumina's protocols, and cDNA libraries were constructed using TruSeq™ Stranded Total RNA Library Prep Kit reagents (Majorbio Bio-pharm Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China). All processes were conducted according to the manufacturer's instructions, followed by sequencing on an Illumina HiSeq platform at Majorbio Bio-pharm Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data analysis, differentially expressed genes (DEGs) screening, and bioinformatics analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the expression quantification results, the raw counts were statistically analyzed using DESeq2 (Version 1.24.0) software, and the DEGs were identified as genes with a screening threshold of |log2 (fold change)| ≥ 1 & p value < 0.05. The COG database (http://eggnog.embl.de/) and KEGG database (http://www.genome.jp/kegg/) were used for DEGs annotation. The raw data of RNA-sequencing has been deposited in the NCBI Sequence Read Archive (SRA) database and accession to cite for these SRA data: PRJNA893544.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metabolite extraction, LC‒MS-based untargeted metabolomics analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The sample preparation for the metabolomic analysis was performed as described in 2.3.1, and metabolomics analysis was performed under the same conditions for each group of six biological replicates (control: EC1, EC2, EC3, EC4, EC5, EC6, experimental: ED1, ED2, ED3, ED4, ED5, ED6). The metabolites were extracted with a mixture of 400 μL methanol (80%) containing 0.02 mg/mL L-2 chlorophenylalanine. The obtained samples were ground with a frozen tissue grinder (−10 °C, 50 Hz) for 6 min, and then treated with ultrasound (5 °C, 40 kHz) for 30 min. Subsequently, the samples were stored at −20 °C for 30 min and then centrifuged at 13,000×g for 15 min (4 °C). The supernatant obtained was analyzed through liquid chromatography‒mass spectrometry (LC‒MS, Vanquish Horizon syst, Q-Exactive, Thermo Scientific, USA) by Majorbio Bio-pharm Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data analysis, differentially expressed metabolites (DEMs) screening, and bioinformatics analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The statistical analysis of the raw data files was performed using Progenesis QI (Waters Corporation, Milford, USA) software, and metabolites were identified through a metabolic database (http://www.hmdb.ca/, https://metlin.scripps.edu/), and these metabolites were annotated using the KEGG database (http://www.genome.jp/kegg/). Ropls (R packages) (Version 1.6.2) software was used to obtain the differential metabolites, and metabolites with p value < 0.05 & VIP >1 were considered DEMs.

The height profile of the GQDs was determined through AFM, the diagram showed an image of a 1.5 – 1.8-nm-thick single layer, and the prepared GQDs were spherical particles with good monodispersity and solubility (Fig. 1 a–b). The zeta potential results showed that the synthesized GQDs surface charge was approximately −25.5 mV (Fig. 1c). FTIR spectra were obtained to further elucidate the structural properties of the GQDs (Fig. 1d), the peak at approximately 1617 cm−1 denoted the presence of the CO of the amide or carboxylic group, while the peak at approximately 3465 cm−1 was attributed to O–H stretching in the GQDs, while the 1088 cm−1 peak was related to the C–O alkoxy group. The FTIR spectra of the GQDs showed that the surface of the GQDs possesses hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Naik et al., 2017). In addition, the surface chemistry of the GQDs was also determined through XPS. The deconvoluted N 1s spectra of the GQDs comprised pyridinic N (398.53 eV), pyrrolic N (400.24 eV), and quaternary N (401.0 eV). The C 1s and N 1s spectra of the GQDs evidenced O 1s-C 1s and N 1s-C 1s ratios of approximately 29.05% and 4.53%, respectively (Fig. 1e–h). The characterization results indicated that the GQDs were prepared successfully.

The antibacterial effect of the GQDs was quantified by a standard colony counting method, and the Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli MC1061 and Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus ATCC6538 were used as the experimental templates in this study. As shown in Fig. 2 , the antibacterial properties of the GQDs showed a concentration-dependent relationship. Especially when the concentration of the GQDs reached 400 μg mL−1, the antibacterial rates against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) reached 98.01% and 99.30%, respectively (Fig. 2a–d.). Consequently, the concentration of the GQDs (400 μg mL−1) was chosen for further experimentation.

The morphological alterations in E.coli were observed using SEM to investigate the antibacterial mode of the GQDs, as shown in Figure S4a, the E. coli cells without the GQDs treatment maintained a typical rod shape with blunt rounded ends and a smooth surface, and the E. coli in the control group had the characteristic morphology with aggregation, regularity, and uniformity. However, an abnormal phenomenon after incubation with the GQDs was visible, and the morphology exhibited a rough and wrinkled appearance (Figure S4b). Graphene oxide nanosheets were experimentally and theoretically shown to induce the destructive extraction of phospholipids from E. coli and degrade the cell membranes of E. coli and reduce its viability (Tu et al., 2013). Overall, the considerable damage to the membrane structure and shrinking of the cells indicated loss of the membrane integrity and blockage of cell growth. Furthermore, the disruption of cell membrane structure can further lead to metabolic dysregulation and even the inactivation of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Global transcriptomic and metabolomic responses of E. coli to GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: High-throughput omics profiles of the bacterial transcriptional and metabolic status provided a comprehensive picture of the E. coli response to GQDs stress at the molecular level. The results revealed that the differential expression of 939 genes was significant when exposed to 400 μg mL−1 GQDs, including 487 up-regulated and 452 down-regulated genes (Fig. 3 a). According to the COG database, the GQDs-exposed E. coli showed a remarkable overexpression of 7 genes associated with defense mechanisms and a significant reduction of 10 genes related to intracellular trafficking, secretion, and vesicular transport (Figure S1). Moreover, 10 genes involved in biofilm formation were significantly down-regulated based on the KEGG annotation database. Simultaneously, 116 significantly expressed DEMs were monitored, including 30 up-regulated and 86 down-regulated DEMs (Fig. 3b), and among them, metabolites with map numbers were annotated to the KEGG pathways (Figure S3). From the above, we could understand the functional distribution of the DEGs and DEMs at the macroscopic level and further predict the potential molecular regulations of E. coli under GQDs stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs up-regulated transcripts involved in defense mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: In general, GQDs induce the generation of toxic ROS including singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radical anion (•O2 −) to destroy E. coli (Kuo et al., 2017). Seven genes (mdlB, mdtA, dinF, mdtD, mdtB, mdtC, and baeS) involved in defense mechanisms were found to exhibit various degrees of overexpression in E.coli cells in the GQDs-treated group (Fig. 4 a–b, Table S1). For example, the expression of the dinF gene coding the DinF protein, which has been proven to contribute to protecting bacteria from the oxidative stress of bile salts by expelling ROS (Rodríguez-Beltrán et al., 2012; Lee et al., 2019), was increased 3.833-fold. The exposure of E. coli to GQDs may result in the activation of the SOS gene dinF, which is responsible for the excretion of toxic ROS. Consequently, it is plausible to infer that the response regulator dinF gene may be involved in the regulation of GQDs-induced oxidative damage and counteracting intracellular toxic components.

The two-component system BaeSR is an important extracytoplasmic stress response system in E. coli. Its function is to adapt to changing environmental conditions and stresses (Hu et al., 2020), and the expression level of baeS was significantly up-regulated 4.88-fold after the GQDs-treatment. More importantly, the primary function of the BaeSR response system is to regulate the expression level of genes related to efflux pumps (Cho et al., 2021). Efflux pumps in Gram-negative organisms are involved in cellular homeostasis, intercellular signaling, and stress adaptation, pathogenicity and virulence (Alav et al., 2021). The transcription of the mdtABCD gene cluster, a multidrug resistance cluster, in E. coli was activated by the baeSR two-component system by transferring phosphoryl groups between sensor kinase and response regulator proteins (Baranova and Nikaido, 2002). Therefore, the antibacterial agent GQDs and toxic ROS may be pumped out of E. coli cells. Thus, we deduced that the overexpression of mdtABCD conferred E.coli resistance to GQDs. Simultaneously, the content of isonicotinic acid associated with xenobiotics biodegradation and metabolism was decreased by 0.89-fold (Figure S5; Table S2) in the metabolomic analysis, which may induce the overexpression of genes associated with efflux pumps. Therefore, E. coli could promote its adaptability and resistance to GQDs stress mainly by increasing the expression of genes involved in defense mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs down-regulated transcripts involved in biofilm formation
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to inducing damage to bacterial cell wall/membrane permeability and integrity, the prepared GQDs with negative surface potential suppress bacterial adhesion by virtue of strong electrostatic repulsion, thereby inhibiting the formation of bacterial biofilms (Zhu et al., 2019). The transcriptional levels of ten genes (pdeR, dgcM, flhD, uvrY, fliZ, fliA, yedQ, mlrA, gcvR, and malP) related to biofilm formation, including quorum sensing, flagellar assembly, and bacterial chemotaxis, were significantly down-regulated in the E. coli cells in the GQDs treatment group (Fig. 5 a–b, Table S1). Flagellar movement enables E. coli to obtain resources and avoid toxic substances (Du et al., 2020). An important mode of antagonistic regulation of motility and adhesion is associated with the flagellar master regulator FlhDC (Horne et al., 2016). The effect of GQDs on the biofilm formation pathway of E. coli is shown in Fig. 5b. Notably, the transcription of the flhDC promoter is dependent on the presence of sigma factor FliA (Clarke and Sperandio, 2005), and the gene fliA codes RNA polymerase sigma factor FliA. Additionally, the gene fliZ encodes the regulator of sigmaS (δs) factor FliZ, which down-regulates flagellar gene expression by directly binding to a regulatory region of the gene flhDC operon which encodes the flagellar master regulator FlhDC (Pesavento and Hengge, 2012). It is worth mentioning that genes (flhD, fliA, and fliZ) associated with the movement and adhesion of E. coli cells were significantly down-regulated 2.151-fold, 7.874-fold, and 2.695-fold, respectively. Meanwhile, the gene mlrA, a newly defined positive transcriptional regulator related to extracellular matrix production in E. coli (Brown et al., 2001), was also significantly down-regulated. Furthermore, uvrY gene coding for invasion response regulator UvrY was down-regulated 2.48-fold, and previous publications demonstrated that mutations in UvrY homologues lead to defects in the regulation of bacterial motility genes (Goodier and Ahmer, 2001). Thus, we speculated that GQDs may reduce the motility of E. coli, thereby inhibiting microcolony formation. Furthermore, the metabolomic analysis revealed a reduced expression of l-aspartic acid (C4H7NO4), which is responsible for bacterial chemotaxis (Figure S5; Table S2). Proteins consisting of amino acids with nitrogen-containing groups may induce hydrogen-bonding interactions with other substances, suggesting that GQDs stress may inhibit the motility of bacterial cells. In summary, most transcriptional regulators involved in biofilm formation were remarkably down-regulated, and GQDs have the potential to inhibit the growth of E. coli biofilms.

In addition, GQDs disrupted the synthesis of biofilm self-protection and mutual adhesion extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), which are composed of proteins, polysaccharides, extracellular DNA (eDNA), and to a lesser extent lipids. For example, there is a prominent 2.11-fold decrease in the expression level of yedQ, which encodes active diguanylate cyclase [EC:2.7.7.65] to reduce eDNA, and early biofilm formation (Sanchez-Torres et al., 2011). Interestingly, the expression level of the gene malP encoding glycogen phosphorylase, a rate-limiting enzyme of glycogen degradation, was also reduced by 2.06-fold, leading to disturbed glycogen metabolism in E. coli cells (Strydom et al., 2017). Surprisingly, reduced expression of deoxythymidine diphosphate-l-rhamnose was detected by metabolomic analysis, which contributed to the biosynthesis of the O-antigen of lipopolysaccharide, suggesting that GQDs could inhibit the synthesis of biofilms. Hence, we inferred that GQDs treatment contributes to the inhibition of E. coli biofilm formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs regulated transcripts involved in sulfur metabolism and nitrogen metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene family is renowned for its adsorption-related applications such as the removal of aquatic pollutants, including heavy metal ions and organic chemicals (Faysal Hossain et al., 2020). The possible adsorption of sulfates and other cations and anions by GQDs in the culture medium, which could reduce ion bioavailability to E. coli, cannot be excluded. GQDs exposure appeared to up-regulate several sulfur metabolism-associated genes, among these were genes responsible for transmembrane transport of extracellular sulfate cysteine (cysK, cysC, cysN, and cysJ), taurine (tauA, tauB, and tauD), and alkanesulfonates (ssuD) (Fig. 6 a–b, Table S1.). E. coli can employ sulfonates (taurine and alkanesulfonates) as its sole source of sulfur under sulfur starvation conditions. The ssuABCDE locus is known to play a key role in organosulfur uptake in E. coli (Van der Ploeg et al., 2001). Surprisingly, the metabolic analysis revealed that the expression level of l-cysteine decreased 1.05-fold (Figure S5; Table S2), and l-cysteine is the source of redox-active sulfhydryl groups for all bacterial sulfur-containing biomolecules (proteins, glutathione, etc.), and is capable of promoting Fenton reactions and generating damaging hydroxyl radicals, so l-cysteine levels in the cytoplasm must be strictly controlled (Imlay et al., 2015). Thus, metabolomic analysis showed that the GQDs treatment induced a reduction in l-cysteine biosynthesis, suggesting that sulfur metabolism in E. coli is reprogrammed to protect E.coli from oxidative stress under GQDs stress conditions. In addition, the adaptive response of E.coli to nitrogen-limiting is primarily a ‘scavenging response’, for example, the expression level of hcp, a gene encoding hydroxylamine reductase, whose function is to scavenge toxic byproducts of nitrogen metabolism (Wang et al., 2016a), was increased by 2.69-fold. However, compared to the untreated samples, our results revealed that some DEGs related to nitrogen metabolism were down-regulated after exposure to GQDs, such as the narH, narZ, and narU genes involved in nitrate and nitrite uptake and nitrite extrusion, which were substantially down-regulated by 2.137-fold, 2.865-fold, and 4-fold, respectively (Table S1). Correspondingly, the metabolomics analysis showed a significant decrease in the expression of l-glutamate (Figure S5; Table S2). l-glutamate plays a fundamental role in nitrogen metabolism in all living organisms, and the regulation of intracellular glutamate levels has been reported to be potentially critical for the ability of pathogenic slow-growing mycobacteria to grow in vivo (Viljoen et al., 2013). Overall, GQDs may induce an impaired homeostasis of nitrogen metabolism and sulfur metabolism in E. coli. The metabolic reprogramming observed in E. coli treated with GQDs is typically an adaptive strategy applied to ensure a rapid energy supply and lessen oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs up-regulated transcripts involved in bacterial secretion system
ce:section.ce:para: A molecular dynamics simulation demonstrated that all small-sized GQDs with different shapes translocated through the lipid membrane at a nanosecond timescale, and GQDs entered cells through spontaneous membrane penetration at edge asperities and corner sites (Kong et al., 2021). The dynamic control of protein synthesis, folding, and degradation is an essential cornerstone of bacterial adaptation to different environmental conditions (Song et al., 2021). Ten genes (ychE, secB, secA, spy, cecR, secY, yadC, glmM, ffh, and uidB) involved in bacterial secretion system (intracellular trafficking, secretion, and vesicular transport) showed prominent overexpression after the treatment with GQDs (Fig. 7 a–b, Table S1). Notably, three genes associated with the Sec protein secretion system, secA, secB, and secY, were observably up-regulated 2.76-fold, 2.05-fold, and 2.50-fold, respectively. The general function of the protein-export chaperone SecB is to ensure correct interactions within and between other polypeptides, including proper folding and assembly (Crane and Randall, 2017). The secA gene encodes the preprotein translocation ATPase SecA, which promotes the secretion of periplasmic proteins in E. coli, and the targeting effect of SecB requires specific binding to SecA (Wang et al., 2019a). In addition, SecA binds the cytosolic loops of SecY with high-affinity, and SecA ATPase interacts synergistically with the SecYEG translocon to secrete proteins into the periplasmic space in E. coli (Oswald et al., 2021). Additionally, the expression level of the ffh gene, which is required for viability and efficient protein export, was up-regulated by 2.06-fold. A signal recognition particle consisting of only one polypeptide (Ffh) and one 4.5S RNA is responsible for targeting secretory and membrane proteins in the inner membrane of E. coli (Phillips and Silhavy, 1992). Taken together, we predicted that GQDs exacerbated the protein secretion process in E. coli cells, and the proteostasis network in E. coli may be disturbed.

Moreover, negatively charged GQDs lead to changes in the peptidoglycan structure on the cell wall due to electrostatic repulsion, enhancing cell permeability and releasing soluble ions and proteins, leading to microbial lysis or death. The gene glmM codes phosphoglucosamine mutase GlmM, which catalyzes the interconversion of glucosamine-6-phosphate into glucosamine-1-phosphate and subsequently to UDP-N-acetylglucosamine, the latter product serves as a precursor for bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan and outer membrane lipopolysaccharides synthesis (Reuse et al., 1997). Meanwhile, a substantial 18.23-fold up-regulation of the gene spy, which encodes a newly identified molecular chaperone, Spy, suppresses protein aggregation and assists in protein refolding in an ATP-independent manner in E. coli periplasm (Ruan et al., 2020). Specifically, overexpression of spy contributes to restoring the peptidoglycan biosynthesis pathway by preventing envelope proteins aggregation (Kouidmi et al., 2018). Hence, the membrane damage induced by GQDs may activate the cell membrane wall repair system in E. coli, which suggests that GQDs could act as a cell wall-activating (cell membrane targeting) agent. On the contrary, some amino acids (l-isoleucine, choline, and glutathione), and nucleotides (uridine and guanosine) were secreted with a trend of down-regulation in metabolic analysis (Figure S5; Table S2). Gram-negative bacterial proteins exported from the cytosol to the external environment by the Sec-secretion system are also known as autotransporters, and the dysregulation of their metabolic expression may cause and provide an explanation for the irreversible bacterial cell injury and loss of viability. Overall, intracellular bacterial secretion homeostasis was disrupted by the GQDs, which could be a novel attack strategy of GQDs.

A line of evidence concerning the molecular regulatory mechanisms of E. coli triggered by GQDs was revealed in this study. Taking advantage of integrated transcriptomic and metabolomics, the results reported a total of 939 DEGs and 116 DEMs were obviously differentially expressed. Based on the gene-metabolite network, GQDs induced reduced biofilm formation and disturbed of sulfur metabolism and nitrogen metabolism in E. coli. Furthermore, the damaged E. coli cells displayed selective genes expression to resist GQDs stress and survive. Collectively, this study greatly enhanced our understanding of GQDs-induced inactivation mechanism of E. coli cells at the multiomics level. GQDs exert their antibacterial activity via different molecular targets, which provides new ideas for future studies designing GQDs with high antibacterial activity.

Yan Fang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing. Wanfeng Wu: Investigation, Validation. Yan Zhao: Validation. Haoqiang Liu: Validation. Zongda Li: Software. Xinbo Li: Software. Minwei Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yanan Qin: Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No: 31960496), National key R & D projects (2019YFC1606100) and Autonomous Region Key S & T Special Projects (NO. 2022A02009-3).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Negatively charged carbon nanodots with bacteria resistance ability for high-performance antibiofilm formation and anticorrosion coating design
Small
